title: Recoverable terminalization and atomic artifacts
goal: Turn terminalization into an invariant-bearing recoverable protocol and make authoritative JSON artifact replacement crash-safe, so mandatory persistence failures can never be discarded before cleanup.
acceptance: Use behavioral TDD: first add fault-injection tests at ArtifactStore/StateStore seams and daemon lifecycle tests that fail because terminal summary/update errors are currently discarded, then implement without loosening them.

Authoritative JSON writes use same-directory temporary files, flush/sync as appropriate, atomic rename, and parent-directory durability; a crash or injected failure leaves either the old complete artifact or the new complete artifact, never truncated JSON.
Terminalization is an explicit protocol with durable intent, atomic terminal summary replacement, transactional terminal status plus event commit, notification bookkeeping, and cleanup in that order.

A mandatory summary/state/event persistence failure cannot be ignored and cannot yield a falsely terminal run. It leaves durable retry/recovery evidence and preserves the primary requested terminal outcome and failure cause.
Daemon startup and explicit resume reconcile incomplete terminalization idempotently without duplicate terminal events, notifications, close records, publication commits, or cleanup side effects.
Cleanup and visibility-notification failures remain non-authoritative incidents after mandatory state is durable; they never overwrite the primary terminal reason.
Tests inject failures before temp write, before rename, after rename, during status/event commit, during notification, and during cleanup, then prove restart reconciliation and valid artifacts.
The protocol is extracted only as a deep terminalization/artifact seam; manager.rs is not split by phase or file size.
must_ask_if: The design would advertise a terminal status before the matching durable summary and event exist.
Recovery would require guessing from renderer output or Herdr state rather than daemon-owned records.
Atomic replacement cannot be implemented portably without changing the documented runtime support policy.
An existing lifecycle or notification regression would need to be weakened.
